one_line_claim: AUDITS is a 530K-image benchmark for image manipulation localization that lets researchers stress-test detectors across image source, manipulation type, manipulation size, and human-perceived edit quality.
what_they_tested: They build two subsets from VisualNews and COCO, generate diffusion-based replacement, removal, and insertion edits with 11 manipulation techniques, add masks and pristine images, and evaluate PSCC-Net, MMFusion, HiFi, EVP, TruFor, SIDA, DOLOS, UperNet, plus several domain-generalization variants. The core experiments train on AUDITS-News or AUDITS-COCO, test in-domain and cross-domain across manipulation-type ID/OOD splits, then add size-conditioned results, external transfer to MagicBrush and CocoGLIDE, and a small AMT-based quality analysis over 3,412 majority-voted images.
strongest_point: The benchmark design is genuinely useful: it separates image-source shift from manipulation-type shift, includes modern diffusion/inpainting generators rather than only classic splicing or copy-move artifacts, evaluates many existing detectors under the same protocol, and reports enough cross-source/cross-generator failures to expose brittle generalization that single-dataset reporting would hide.
weakest_point: The dataset is large but still synthetic and mask-defined: most edits are generated from COCO/VisualNews segmentation masks, VisualNews masks come from ODISE rather than human ground truth, the evaluation invents an ambiguous-pixel weighting rule with empirical thresholds, and the human-quality study covers only a small subset of generators and images, so the benchmark may measure detector response to this construction pipeline as much as real-world manipulation robustness.
missing_baselines: A direct comparison to training on existing large manipulation datasets such as COCO-Inpaint, TGIF, DOLOS, MagicBrush, and DEFACTO under the same train/test budget, not only external evaluation after AUDITS training or the limited EVP-only DEFACTO appendix.

A simple image-difference or artifact heuristic baseline using the original/edited pair where available, to calibrate how much of the benchmark is driven by diffusion off-mask drift and global color/texture changes.

Modern foundation-model or forensic-feature baselines that are not standard supervised localization networks, such as CLIP/DINO feature anomaly maps, noise-residual detectors, or self-supervised change/localization methods.
Human localization or human image-level detection baselines on the same split, since the paper repeatedly motivates social trust and obvious edits but only uses humans to label perceived quality.
Cross-dataset training baselines where detectors are trained on mixtures of AUDITS plus traditional and diffusion datasets for all main model families, not only EVP in appendix Tables 11 and 12.

Compute- and data-matched baselines showing whether the performance drops are caused by source/generator shift rather than unequal manipulation-size distributions or class/object frequency differences between COCO and VisualNews.
missing_ablations: Sensitivity of the ternary evaluation to the off-mask drift threshold tau=0.0025 and ambiguous-pixel weight alpha=0.5; these choices directly affect AUROC/F1 but are only described as empirical.
Replacing ODISE-predicted masks on VisualNews with manually checked masks or high-confidence subsets to estimate how much annotation noise affects localization scores.
Generator-by-generator train/test leave-one-out results for all 11 manipulation methods in the main paper; Tables 24 and 25 help, but they are not integrated into the primary protocol.
Ablation of same-object replacement versus different-object replacement at realistic scale; the current new-object experiment uses only 100 images per subset.
Quality-label robustness to survey design choices, annotator filtering, worker agreement thresholds, majority-vote rules, and the decision to merge 'maybe' with 'no'.
Per-source and per-topic VisualNews analysis beyond aggregate News vs COCO, especially because news outlets and topics are unbalanced.
A study that equalizes manipulation-size bins across COCO and News before attributing failures to image-source shift.
Evaluation with pristine/authentic images should be part of the main protocol, not relegated to an appendix table whose interpretation is acknowledged as confusing.
red_flags: The paper's strongest practical framing is misinformation robustness, but all manipulated images are generated in a controlled synthetic pipeline over COCO and VisualNews; real edited social/news imagery, screenshots, compression chains, reshares, and mixed editing workflows are not central to the benchmark.
AUDITS-News ground-truth masks depend on ODISE panoptic segmentation, so mask errors and open-vocabulary segmentation biases can become benchmark labels.

The ambiguous-pixel protocol rewards or penalizes off-mask diffusion drift using fixed tau and alpha values chosen empirically, yet the main source-domain and manipulation-type conclusions rely on this nonstandard metric in Sections 4.2 and 4.3.
The human evaluation is small relative to the dataset: 4,950 surveyed images and 3,412 quality-labeled images out of 530,640 total, with Adobe Firefly excluded and some generators missing for COCO.
The paper says most people realized images were manipulated in the survey, which weakens the claim that the benchmark represents highly convincing manipulations.

Domain-generalization methods are treated as potential fixes, but the paper gives limited implementation detail and no careful tuning/fairness analysis, so the negative DG conclusion should be read cautiously.

Several tables are hard to parse in the extracted text and the paper sometimes uses awkward or inconsistent wording around train/test directions, authentic-image inclusion, and performance interpretation.
The ethics section is thin: it asserts the work is ethical by nature and only briefly notes misuse, despite releasing a large manipulated-image dataset.
positive_signals: The dataset is publicly linked on Hugging Face, which makes this much more valuable than a purely descriptive benchmark paper.
AUDITS explicitly separates COCO and VisualNews, enabling a cleaner test of image-source shift than prior benchmarks that confound source and manipulation method.
The benchmark includes 11 recent manipulation techniques across replacement, removal, and insertion, including SDXL, Flux-Inpainting, HD-Painter, GLIGEN, and Adobe Firefly.
The main evaluation covers a broad detector set: PSCC-Net, MMFusion, HiFi, EVP, TruFor, SIDA, DOLOS, UPerNet, and domain-generalization variants.
The results are not just leaderboard numbers; they identify concrete failure modes such as large manipulation recall problems, source shift, generator shift, and object-category-specific weakness.
External transfer experiments on MagicBrush and CocoGLIDE show that method rankings change across datasets, supporting the paper's argument against single-benchmark generalization claims.
The appendix adds useful checks with authentic images, seed statistics, semantic salience via captions, traditional manipulation datasets, image-level detection, and recent generator breakdowns.
The paper is honest that current DG methods do not solve the multi-axis shifts and that future work needs contextual information and better manipulations.
verdict: Worth reviewing for Paper Radio as a benchmark episode rather than an algorithmic breakthrough. The paper has a strong story: detectors look competent in-distribution, then break under source, generator, size, and external-dataset shifts. The critique should focus on whether AUDITS measures real manipulation robustness or mostly robustness to a particular synthetic diffusion-and-segmentation pipeline, and on how much the nonstandard ambiguous-pixel metric and small human-quality subset shape the conclusions.
citations: data/papers/arxiv-2605.20174.md:1-22 gives the title, metadata, abstract, 530K-image claim, public PDF/fulltext paths, and triage rationale.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:20-26 states the core AUDITS claim: over 530K images, two image sources, diffusion-based inpaintings, and domain-shift experiments.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:53-72 lists the four intended axes: image source, manipulation quality, manipulation type, and manipulation size, and claims DG methods do not fix the shifts.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:103-121 compares AUDITS to prior datasets and reports 530,640 images, multi-domain coverage, diffusion manipulations, controlled sizes, 11 manipulation types, and human perception labels.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:162-168 reports AUDITS-News with 311,989 images and AUDITS-COCO with 218,651 images, including manipulated and pristine counts.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:174-207 describes the 11 manipulation techniques and the use of ODISE masks for VisualNews and COCO masks for COCO.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:215-244 describes VisualNews topic/outlet diversity, COCO object categories, and manipulation-size counts for small, medium, and large edits.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:228-252 describes the AMT quality survey: 4,950 images, 14,850 responses, 1,829 workers, and 3,412 majority-voted high/low quality images.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:262-272 lists the evaluated models and domain-generalization variants, including PSCC-Net, MMFusion, HiFi, EVP, TruFor, SIDA, DOLOS, UPerNet, SWAD, Model Soups, MIRO, and URM.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:286-338 defines the ternary ground truth, off-mask drift threshold tau=0.0025, and ambiguous-pixel weight alpha=0.5.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:343-380 gives Table 2 and notes that the ambiguous-pixel weighted protocol is used only for Sections 4.2 and 4.3, while later tables use standard metrics.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:387-405 reports the main source/type-shift conclusion and says DG variants give only modest, inconsistent gains.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:412-423 reports that small manipulations can score higher partly because most pixels are authentic, and that large manipulations suffer from low recall.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:425-460 reports external transfer to MagicBrush and CocoGLIDE and argues that method rankings are not fixed across datasets.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:471-490 reports the high/low human-quality AUC comparison and says quality labels have negligible impact on detector performance.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:498-508 reports object-category qualitative analysis and the conclusion that current detectors struggle on OOD samples.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:713-723 gives the ethical caveat and explains why authentic-image results are moved to appendix Table 6.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:788-797 reports the traditional-manipulation analysis showing AUDITS alone does poorly on classic manipulations but AUDITS plus DEFACTO can help.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:865-882 describes small-scale new-object replacement and additional human-evaluation findings, including that many survey participants detected manipulation and that AUDITS-COCO was more challenging.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.20174.txt:994-1007 gives recent-generator AUC breakdowns for Flux-Inpainting, HD-Inpainting, Firefly, and SDXL when trained on AUDITS-News or AUDITS-COCO.
